skill.name: it-operations
skill.description: Guides IT operations work across monitoring, incident response, infrastructure management, automation, and backup recovery using practical runbooks and decision frameworks.
skill.summary: Plan, operate, monitor, automate, and recover IT services with structured operations guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential-access patterns. Review confirms the skill is documentation-focused, but it contains copyable privileged operations, destructive storage commands, secret-handling examples, and automation patterns that require human remediation before marketplace publication.

high_findings.title: Copyable Privileged Infrastructure Commands
high_findings.description: The skill includes operational examples for sudo access, systemd enablement, user account creation, package installation, and service control. These are legitimate IT operations tasks, but publishing them without stronger guardrails can cause privilege changes or production outages if copied blindly.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit privileged administration workflows. They appear operational rather than malicious, but the impact of misuse is high.

high_findings.title: Destructive Storage Operations in Runbook Examples
high_findings.description: The infrastructure guide provides copyable disk and filesystem commands for LVM setup, including filesystem creation on block devices. These commands can erase data if run against the wrong device and need warnings, placeholders, and verification steps.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly create filesystems on device paths. This is normal for provisioning but destructive when applied to existing disks.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Credential and System File Handling
high_findings.description: The references include SSH authorized key paths, certificate file paths, Windows SAM references, /etc/passwd parsing, API key placeholders, and cloud secret examples. Some are legitimate documentation, but the content normalizes access to sensitive materials without enough handling guidance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show sensitive paths and secret placeholders. The examples are plausible operations guidance, but the security handling is incomplete.

medium_findings.title: External Automation Patterns Require Input Controls
medium_findings.description: The automation reference includes subprocess and Ansible command examples, including Slack-triggered operations. Some examples whitelist services, but host and command execution patterns still need stronger validation and approval controls before publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is visible and partly controlled. Legitimate use is likely, but the marketplace copy-and-run context increases risk.

medium_findings.title: Webhook and Incident API Examples Need Secret Hygiene
medium_findings.description: Monitoring and incident examples include Slack webhook and PagerDuty API key placeholders. The examples are not live credentials, but users may copy the pattern and store secrets directly in configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders, so this is not credential leakage. The risk is unsafe implementation guidance for secret storage.

low_findings.title: Ransomware and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Contextual
low_findings.description: Static findings for ransomware and command-and-control terms appear in disaster recovery, cloud infrastructure, and security monitoring context. I found no evidence that the skill instructs extortion, persistence, or unauthorized control.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The nearby text frames these terms as resilience, cloud, or DR content. Full-file intent appears benign, but the static signal is still worth noting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Mostly Reflect Monitoring Metrics and Examples
low_findings.description: Several weak cryptography hits are caused by acronyms such as SLA, SLO, SHA, or placeholder examples rather than a clear recommendation to deploy weak algorithms. No evidence found of malicious cryptographic downgrade intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are general operations terminology. Some later examples may still need cleanup, but the broad weak-crypto signal is overinclusive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and Subprocess Execution in Operational Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly provides shell, subprocess, Ansible, and service-management examples. These are expected for IT operations but need explicit least-privilege, dry-run, approval, and test-environment safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is repeatedly present and operationally powerful. The examples are not obviously malicious, but they can change production systems.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Access to Sensitive Paths and Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples reference SSH directories, certificate files, system account databases, /etc/passwd, API keys, webhooks, and cloud credentials. Marketplace publication should require safer secret-management language and redacted placeholders.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited material clearly touches sensitive resources. The risk depends on user execution context, so confidence is high but not critical.

content.value_statement: Operations teams need consistent runbooks for incidents, monitoring, infrastructure, automation, and recovery. This skill provides structured guidance that helps teams standardize daily operations and reduce service risk.
actual_capabilities: Defines monitoring, alerting, SLI, SLO, and SLA planning workflows.
Provides incident severity models, response roles, escalation paths, and post-incident review templates.
Guides infrastructure operations, server lifecycle planning, capacity review, and change management.
Explains automation approaches for runbooks, configuration management, orchestration, and toil reduction.
Documents backup, recovery, disaster recovery, RPO, RTO, and continuity planning practices.
Compares common operations tools and frameworks across ITIL, SRE, observability, and cloud operations.
limitations: It is guidance and documentation, not a tested automation package.
Some examples require privileged access and must be reviewed before execution.
Tool names, cloud commands, and version references may need updates for each environment.
It does not replace security review, change approval, or production validation.

use_cases.title: Standardize Incident Response
use_cases.description: Create consistent severity levels, escalation paths, stakeholder updates, and post-incident review practices for an operations team.

use_cases.title: Plan Monitoring Coverage
use_cases.description: Design service indicators, dashboards, alert thresholds, and on-call workflows for production systems.

use_cases.title: Improve Recovery Readiness
use_cases.description: Document backup strategies, recovery objectives, DR testing, and continuity plans for critical services.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a runbook for [service name]. Include service purpose, owners, dependencies, health checks, common alerts, escalation contacts, and safe rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Alerting Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these alerts for [service or platform]. Classify each alert as page, ticket, dashboard-only, or remove. Explain the threshold and response owner.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an Incident Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a blameless post-incident review for this incident timeline: [timeline]. Include impact, root cause, contributing factors, action items, owners, and due dates.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Operations Improvement Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess our current operations model: [context]. Recommend a 90-day plan covering monitoring, incident response, automation, backup testing, change management, risks, and success metrics.

output_examples.input: Design an incident workflow for a customer-facing payment outage.
output_examples.output: A severity classification, incident commander role, communication cadence, technical workstream, rollback checkpoint, customer update plan, and post-incident review outline.

output_examples.input: Create a monitoring plan for a new API service.
output_examples.output: A service-level indicator list, dashboard sections, alert thresholds, log and trace requirements, escalation routing, and weekly review metrics.

output_examples.input: Prepare a disaster recovery test plan for the database tier.
output_examples.output: A scoped DR exercise with RPO and RTO targets, prerequisites, restore validation steps, rollback criteria, evidence capture, and follow-up actions.

best_practices: Review every command example in a non-production environment before operational use.
Replace all placeholder secrets with managed secret storage and least-privilege access.
Tie runbooks to owners, approval steps, rollback criteria, and regular review dates.
anti_patterns: Copying privileged commands into production without validation or change approval.
Embedding API keys, webhook URLs, or cloud credentials directly in scripts or configuration.
Treating generic runbooks as complete without adapting them to local systems and controls.

faq.question: Is this skill an automation tool?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation and guidance skill with example workflows and commands.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Only after review, testing, change approval, and adaptation to your environment.

faq.question: Does it cover incident response?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes severity models, escalation, roles, communication, and post-incident review guidance.

faq.question: Does it help with monitoring design?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers observability principles, metrics, alerting, dashboards, and service objectives.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The content includes privileged commands, destructive storage examples, and sensitive secret-handling patterns.

faq.question: What should be fixed before publication?
faq.answer: Add stronger safety warnings, remove risky copyable commands, and improve secret-management guidance.
